paper_title: Coarse-Guided Visual Generation via Weighted h-Transform Sampling

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Coarse-guided visual generation, which synthesizes fine visual samples from degraded or low-fidelity coarse references, is essential for various real-world applications. While training-based approaches are effective, they are inherently limited by high training costs and restricted generalization due to paired data collection. Accordingly, recent training-free works propose to leverage pretrained diffusion models and incorporate guidance during the sampling process. However, these training-free methods either require knowing the forward (fine-to-coarse) transformation operator, e.g., bicubic downsampling, or are difficult to balance between guidance and synthetic quality. To address these challenges, we propose a novel guided method by using the h-transform, a tool that can constrain stochastic processes (e.g., sampling process) under desired conditions. Specifically, we modify the transition probability at each sampling timestep by adding to the original differential equation with a drift function, which approximately steers the generation toward the ideal fine sample. To address unavoidable approximation errors, we introduce a noise-level-aware schedule that gradually de-weights the term as the error increases, ensuring both guidance adherence and high-quality synthesis. Extensive experiments across diverse image and video generation tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of our method.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Coarse-Guided Visual Generation via Weighted h-Transform Sampling Yanghao Wang, Ziqi Jiang, Zhen Wang, and Long Chen The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology ywangtg@connect.ust.hk, zhenwang@ust.hk, longchen@ust.hk https://github.com/HKUST-LongGroup/Coarse-guided-Gen 6 2 0 2 2 ] . [ Fig. 1: Given coarse visual sample (left one in each pair) as the guidance, our method generates its corresponding refined result (right one) in training-free manner. Abstract. Coarse-guided visual generation, which synthesizes fine visual samples from degraded or low-fidelity coarse references, is essential for various real-world applications. While training-based approaches are effective, they are inherently limited by high training costs and restricted generalization due to paired data collection. Accordingly, recent trainingfree works propose to leverage pretrained diffusion models and incorporate guidance during the sampling process. However, these training-free methods either require knowing the forward (fine-to-coarse) transformation operator, e.g., bicubic downsampling, or are difficult to balance between guidance and synthetic quality. To address these challenges, we propose novel guided method by using the h-transform, tool that can constrain stochastic processes (e.g., sampling process) under desired conditions. Specifically, we modify the transition probability at each sampling timestep by adding to the original differential equation with drift function h, which approximately steers the generation toward the ideal fine sample. To address unavoidable approximation errors, we introduce 1 7 5 0 2 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen noise-level-aware schedule that gradually de-weights the term as the error increases, ensuring both guidance adherence and high-quality synthesis. Extensive experiments across diverse image and video generation tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of our method. Keywords: Guided Visual Generation Diffusion Model Doobs hTransform

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Recent advances in diffusion [14, 44] and flow matching [27, 28] models have demonstrated remarkable visual generation capability. Benefited from large-scale pretraining, high-quality samples can be generated by existing unconditional and text-to-visual (T2V) models [23, 39, 49] in the case of without guidance or only with text guidance. However, some real-world applications (e.g., deblurring and super-resolution) always require coarse visual sample to guide synthesis, which these pretrained unconditional and T2V models cannot handle directly. Thus, recent works [57, 19, 21, 31, 41, 44] aim to solve such coarse-guided visual generation task (cf ., Figure 1): given coarse visual sample (e.g., blurred image, or warped video) as the guidance, the model needs to generate the corresponding refined sample (e.g., clear image, or an undistorted video). straightforward approach to coarse-guided visual generation is to train translation networks. As shown in Figure 2(a), previous studies [8, 56, 59] try to train from scratch or finetune translation model with paired coarse-fine data. For different types of coarse sample (e.g., low-resolution images, blurred images, and warped videos), additional/existing models must be trained/finetuned with the corresponding paired data. Consequently, these training-based approaches suffer from inherent high costs (i.e., training and collecting paired data) and limited generalization. Subsequent work resorts to training-free solutions that only modify the sampling process to achieve guided generation. There are two mainstream trainingfree strategies: 1) Solving Inverse Problem [57, 19, 21, 44]: As shown in Figure 2(b), instead of sampling from the marginal distribution, they approximate the posterior probability term and sample directly from the conditional distribution. However, the approximation used in these methods usually relies on known forward fine-to-coarse operator, i.e., the transformation from fine samples to coarse samples must be known, such as bicubic downsampling or Gaussian mask operator. Thus, this prior (known operator) requirement reduces the robustness of these methods, as the operator may be unknown in many scenarios. 2) Start-Guided Synthesis [31, 41]: As shown in Figure 2(c), they first get the sampling start by adding noise to the coarse sample. Then they can sample from it with pretrained diffusion model to get the refined sample. However, the guidance is entirely dependent on setting the noisy coarse sample as the starting point. Adding large amount of noise results in loss of guidance signals, whereas adding small amount of noise yields only limited quality improvement. In that Coarse-guided-Gen 3 Fig. 2: Existing and our solutions. (a) Training translation networks based on paired data, which is costly and non-generalizable to different types of coarse samples. (b) Solving inverse problems based on known forward operator, making it is not robust. (c) Adding noise to the coarse sample and denoising it, which is difficult to balance the guidance and quality. (d) Our method leverages the h-transform to achieve training-free, operator-free, and stable coarse-guided generation. case, this start-dependent guided manner leads to an unstable balance between guidance faithfulness and generation quality. Based on the above discussions, we observe that current solutions are constrained by training, the requirement for known operators, and unstable balance. To this end, inspired by Doobs h-transform [10,38], tool that can constrain the sampling process with the given guidance, we propose novel coarse-guided generation method Weighted h-Transform Sampling. Intuitively, our method incorporates guidance signals during sampling by modifying the transition probability at each time step and finally generates guided refined result. This probability modification can be interpreted as introducing additional traction toward the ideal underlying refined result on top of the original sampling route. Specifically, we achieve traction by adding new drift adjustment hx0=y (x is the time-index updating latent and is the ideal result) to the original predicted score sθ (cf ., Figure 2(d)). By sampling with new score (sθ + hx0=y), the result can be guaranteed to be y. However, this hx0=y is not tractable because 4 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen it depends on knowing the ground truth, i.e., ideal result y. Since we can not directly get hx0=y, we found that we can leverage another tractable hx0= (cid:101)y to approximate it, where (cid:101)y is the given coarse sample. Subsequently, we analyze the approximation error and find it to be negatively correlated with the noise level of x. To mitigate the error influence, we use noise-level-aware schedule to adjust the weight of hx0= (cid:101)y across different time steps. That is, as the approximation error increases, we smoothly decrease the weight of the hx0= (cid:101)y across the whole sampling process. In this way, the generated sample not only adheres to the coarse guidance but also improves in quality. In summary, our contributions can be summarized as follows: We propose Weighted h-Transform Sampling, coarse-guided visual generation method based on Doobs h-transform perspective by deriving an approximation hx0= (cid:101)y to replace the untractable hx0=y. It is training-free, requires no prior knowledge of the forward operator, and is stable. We analyze the approximation error and indicate negative correlation between the noise level and the approximation error. Then, we design smooth, noise-level-aware weight schedule for the approximation term to mitigate the influence of approximation error. Extensive experiments across different image and video generation tasks show our effectiveness and generalization.

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.content: 2.1 Diffusion Models The diffusion models aim to capture the target data distribution p0 by learning transport process from prior distribution pT (e.g., Gaussian distribution) to p0. To achieve that, forward diffusion process from p0 to pT is first determined and can be described with such stochastic differential equation (SDE): dx = f(xt, t)dt + g(t)dw, (1) where [0, ] is the time-index, x0 p0, is the drift function, is the diffusion coefficient, and is Brown motion. Meanwhile, the marginal distribution of xt at time corresponding to this SDE is pt(xt). Then, we have reversed SDE to describe the reversed process (from pT to p0) of the forward process: dx = [f(xt, t) g2(t)xt log pt(xt)]dt + g(t)dw, (2) where is the revered Brown motion. According to the FokkerPlanck equation [30] (F-P Equation), we can convert this reversed SDE into an ordinary differential equation (ODE) that has the same marginal distribution pt(xt): dx = [f(xt, t) 1 g2(t)xt log pt(xt)]dt. (3) Coarse-guided-Gen 5 We can sample synthetic samples by solving the SDE in Eq. (2) or the ODE in Eq. (3). However, xt log pt(xt) (also known as the score) is not known. Thus, parameterized network sθ() with parameters θ can be trained to predict it. The optimization target is: Ext,x0,t (cid:2)sθ(xt, t) xt log pt(xtx0)2 2 (cid:3) , min θ (4) where x0 p0, [0, ] and xt pt(xtx0). xt log pt(xtx0) are tractable since the conditional distribution is defined by the forward SDE in Eq. (1), and it has closed-form solutions. After training, sθ can be used as score predictor to replace xt log pt(xt) for solving Eq. (2) or Eq. (3). 2.2 Doobs h-Transform For given SDE like Eq. (1), the corresponding process can transport x0 p0 to Gaussian noise. However, in some situations, such as image translation, we may need to establish mapping relation between x0 and fixed point y. Thus, Doobs h-transform [10, 38] proposes to modify the probability transition of the original SDE by adding an g2(t)hxT =y term to the original drifting term f: dx = [f(xt, t) + g2(t)hxT =y]dt + g(t)dw, (5) where hxT =y is called the function. The new transition can guarantee that the SDE ends at the fixed point y, where hxT =y is designed as xt log pt(xT = yxt). It can improve the transition probability of pt(xT = yxt) and guarantee xT = by adjusting the drift coefficient at each time step.

sections.title: 3 Weighted h-Transform Sampling
sections.content: Problem Formulation. Assuming that there exists an underlying joint distribution ( (cid:101)y, y) q, which describes the probability measurement between coarse visual sample (cid:101)y and its corresponding fine sample y. For the coarse-guided visual generation task, given coarse sample as the guidance (cid:101)y, our objective is to leverage pretrained diffusion model (a.k.a., the score predictor) sθ to generate its corresponding fine sample. 3.1 Guided Generation with h-Transform For given coarse sample (cid:101)y, if we have its corresponding ideal fine sample y, we can leverage the h-transform (cf ., Section 2.2) to modify the transition kernel of the reversed SDE (Eq. (2)) and guarantee it ends at (i.e., x0 = y) by minusing1 g2(t)hx0=y from the original drifting term: dx = [f(xt, t) g2(t)xt log pt(xt) g2(t)hx0=y)]dt + g(t)dw, 1 Different from the adding in Eq. (5), here the minusing is because Eq. (2) is (6) reversed SDE, i.e., dt < 0. 6 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Fig. 3: Overview of Weighted h-Transform Sampling. (a) If we have hx0=y, the generation result will be the ideal sample. (b) We leverage hx0= (cid:101)y to approximate the untractable hx0=y and derive that the error is increasing gradually during the sampling process. (c) To mitigate the error influence, we decrease the approximation weight and finally generate high-quality refined sample. where hx0=y is xt log pt(x0 = yxt). For any xT , after solving this SDE, the endpoint x0 must be (shown in Figure 3(a)). To simplify the solving process, we can further convert Eq. (6) into its corresponding PF-ODE: dx = [f(xt, t) 1 g2(t)(xt log pt(xt) + hx0=y)]dt. (7) This ODE has the equivalent marginal distribution as Eq. (6)2. Thus, the solution of Eq. (7) is also y. However, its obvious that the hx0=y is not known since is off-course unknown as the ground truth we want to generate. Thus, we can not directly achieve the guided generation using Eq. (7). 3.2 Approximation for the Untractable hx0=y As hx0=y = xt log pt(x0 = yxt) is untractable, we manage to convert it into tractable and appropriate approximation. To this end, we propose to approximate it with the following: hx0=y = xt log pt(x0 = yxt) xt log pt(x0 = (cid:101)yxt). 2 This can be proved by the F-P Equation, and we provide the proof in the Appendix. (8) Coarse-guided-Gen 7 We name xt log pt(x0 = (cid:101)yxt) as hx0= (cid:101)y, which is another function (cf ., Figure 3(b)). Then, we use Bayes rule to change hx0= (cid:101)y into: hx0= (cid:101)y = xt log pt(x0 = (cid:101)yxt) = xt log pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) xt log pt(xt), (9) where the second term xt log pt(xt) can be replaced by the given trained score predictor sθ. Besides, we already known the conditional distribution pt(xtx0): pt(xtx0) = (xt; αtx0, σt 2I), (10) where αt and σt (noise level) are determined by f(xt, t), g2(t) of Eq. (1). When x0 = (cid:101)y, which is also given as the coarse sample, pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) is closed-form as (xt; αt (cid:101)y, σt 2I) and log pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) is tractable: log pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) = xt (cid:18) 1 2σ2 (xt αt (cid:101)y)T(xt αt (cid:101)y) (cid:19) = 1 σ2 (αt (cid:101)y xt). (11) After that, we achieve to convert the untractable hx0=y into the following tractable approximation: hx0=y hx0= (cid:101)y = 1 σ2 (αt (cid:101)y xt) xt log pt(xt). (12) 3.3 Approximation Error Analysis Our proposed approximation in Eq. (8) inevitably introduces approximation errors. We can calculate the Euclidean distance between hx0=y and hx0= (cid:101)y as the approximation error : = log pt(x0 = yxt) log pt(x0 = (cid:101)yxt)2 = log pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) log pt(xtx0 = y)2 = ((cid:101)y y)2. αt σ2 (13) Taking the variance-preserving (VP) diffusion model as the example (α2 1), we can replace αt with σ in Eq. (13) and get: + σ2 = = (cid:112)(1 σ2 ) σ2 ((cid:101)y y)2. (14) We can see that the approximation error is negatively correlated with the noise level σt. Meanwhile, when σt 0, and when σt 1, 0. Y. Chen Algorithm 1 Weighted h-Transform Sampling 1: Input: Coarse visual sample Step size t, Noise schedules αt and σ2 , Weight function λσ (cid:101)y, Pretrained score predictor sθ, Step number , 2: Output: Refined synthetic sample x0 3: = 4: xt (0, I) 5: for = to 1 do 6: 7: 8: end for xtt = xt [f(xt, t) 1 = 2 g2(t)(sθ + λσ ( 1 σ2 (αt (cid:101)y xt) sθ)]t 3.4 Weighted Approximation for Error Restriction According to Eq. (12), we can directly substitute this approximation into the sampling ODE Eq. (7) to get the guided generation result. However, as we discussed in Section 3.3, the approximation error will gradually become unbearable during the sampling process (as σt is getting smaller gradually). To this end, we manage to design weight function for the approximated part to mitigate the influence of the approximation error. When the approximation error is small, the weight can be close to 1. Otherwise, the weight should be decreased gradually to 0. Exactly, we found the noise level σt has good property that satisfies the weight design requirement. Thus, as shown in Figure 3(c), we can use σt-related weight function to adjust the approximation term and get the final sampling ODE: dx = [f(xt, t) 1 2 g2(t)(sθ + λσ ( 1 σ2 (αt (cid:101)y xt) sθ)]dt, (15) where λσ is function of σt (e.g., power function) such that: (1) λσ is negatively correlated with . (2) λσ 1 when 0. (3) λσ 0 when . Intuitively, the hx0=y term can drag the sampling path towards the ideal sample y. However, the hx0=y term is untractable, so we use an hx0= (cid:101)y to approximate it. When the approximation error is small, the traction towards is accurate, so we adopt the approximated hx0= (cid:101)y term more. Otherwise, traction towards is inaccurate, so we adopt the approximated hx0= (cid:101)y term little. Finally, our overall sampling algorithm (take the Euler Solver as an example) is shown in Algorithm 1.

sections.title: 4 Related Work
sections.content: Diffusion Models and Bridges. Diffusion models have recently achieved state-of-the-art visual modeling capability. Although DDPM [14,42], Score matching [43, 44], and Optimal-transport Flow matching [27, 28] provide different perspectives to understand the diffusion family, their success mainly comes from the progressive transport between prior distribution and the target distribution. Coarse-guided-Gen 9 Based on these techniques, various large-scale pre-trained diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion [11, 35, 39], Flux [23, 24], and Wan [49] have emerged and demonstrated remarkable generative performance. In addition to noise-to-visual generation, some works [8, 45, 58, 59] propose diffusion bridges to achieve the visual-to-visual translations. The most typical paradigm is leveraging Doobs htransform [10, 38] to link the paired samples and learn joint distribution to transport between them. Our paper is inspired by the h-transform technique that modifies the probability transition to achieve the guided visual generation. Conditional Visual Generation. Compared with unconditional generation, the conditional generation [1, 15, 55] aims to handle various modality conditions (e.g., text, action, and visual samples) as guidance to constrain the generation range. The text condition is easy to model due to the large-scale text-visual pairs. To this end, current research around conditional generation mainly indicates that the condition is some types of visual signals (e.g., coarse sample, stroke, and pose map). Based on different condition types, the task can be specified into image restoration [25, 37, 51], inverse problem [5, 6, 21, 46], and conditional control [40, 56], and so on. Meanwhile, the solutions also contain training-based [32, 34, 50] and inference-stage guidance [12, 54]. In our paper, we mainly focus on the conditional generation with coarse samples as guidance and generate corresponding high-quality fine samples in training-free manner.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Coarse-Image Guided Generation To demonstrate our effectiveness, we first evaluated on image restoration tasks, fundamental challenge in computer vision that aims to recover high-quality original scenes from degraded, noisy, or incomplete coarse samples. Specifically, this task entails recovering clean image based on given coarse image (cid:101)y. Benchmark. Following DPS [6], we use the validation set of FFHQ 256x256 [18] to serve as the evaluation dataset. This dataset contains 1000 clean images of human faces in 256 256 resolution. For the evaluation metrics, we adopt Frechet Inception Distance [13] (FID) and Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity [57] (LPIPS ) to quantify the effectiveness. FID can indicate the distribution similarity between synthetic images and ground-truth images, while LPIPS focuses on image-level similarity. Implementations. Following the protocol in DPS [6], we transform all clean images by using forward operator and additive noise into coarse images (cid:101)y, i.e., (cid:101)y = A(y)+n. Depending on different forward operators, it can be divided into four image restoration tasks: super-resolution (SR), inpainting (Inpaint), motion deblur (MD), and Gaussian deblur (GD). These coarse images (cid:101)y serve as the guidance in the subsequent generation process. For fair comparison, we follow previous work [6] and use the same pre-trained diffusion model from [9] and 10 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Fig. 4: Qualitative results of coarse-guided image generation. Compared with training-free SDEdit, our method shows more faithful synthesis across tasks. For fairness, we take their own commonly-used hyper-parameter for SDEdit (t0 = 500) and ours (α = 5), and all other settings are the same. maintain other settings the same. During sampling, we set the weight function λσ = σα and report the average result on α {5, 6, 7}. Baselines. We compared our method against: 1) six inverse-problem solutions (cf ., Figure 2(b)): ADMM-TV, ILVR [5, 44], PnP-ADMM [4], MCG [7], DDRM [19], and DPS [6]. These solutions require additional prior for knowing the forward operator. We report their results from [6]. 2) The represented startguided solution (cf ., Figure 2(c)): SDEdit [31], which does not require knowing the forward operator. It has key hyperparameter: the starting timestep t0, which can control the tradeoff between guidance and synthesis quality. For fairness, we report the results averaged on three recommended t0 from the original paper, i.e., t0 {400, 500, 600}, and keep all other settings the same. Results. We gave quantitative comparisons in Table 1. We can see that: 1) Compared with operator-required methods, our approach can outperform most of them and perform competitively against their best one, i.e., DPS. Its worth noting that we achieve it without knowing the forward operator, which is strong prior. 2) Compared with SDEdit, our method can outperform it across six of eight metrics, particularly yielding substantial and consistent improvements in LPIPS. This verifies that our Weighted h-Transform Sampling provides more robust and principled mechanism for coarse-image guidance, leading to superior structural preservation and fidelity. We also gave qualitative comparisons in Figure 4. We can see that our results show better balance between the guidance adherence and image quality than SDEdit. Table 1: Quantitative results of coarse-image guided generation on FFHQ 256x256 validation dataset. Bold: best, underline: second best. Coarse-guided-Gen 11 Method Known Operator ADMM-TV ILVR [5, 44] PnP-ADMM [4] MCG [7] DDRM [19] DPS [6] SDEdit [31] Ours SR Inpaint GD MD FID LPIPS FID LPIPS FID LPIPS FID LPIPS 0.508 110.6 0.657 96.72 0.405 66.52 0.702 87.64 62.15 - 39.35 33.31 0.507 0.403 0.441 0.340 0.332 0.257 0. 0.428 0.563 0.353 0.520 0.294 0.214 0.269 186.7 109.0 90.42 101.2 74.92 44.05 0.463 0.612 0.692 0.286 0.587 152.3 292.2 89.08 310.5 - 181.5 76.54 123.6 29.26 69.71 34.90 38. 0.252 39.92 0.242 0.350 42.35 0.341 52.92 21.19 0.212 0.390 47.24 0.259 44.64 33.28 0.213 Table 2: Quantitative results of camera-controlled video generation on DL3DV. Method GT Coarse Video GWTF(γ = 0.5) GWTF(γ = 0.7) TTM(tw = 4, ts = 8) TTM(tw = 4, ts = 9) Ours MSE - 11.46 26.08 36.45 23.50 23.15 11.45 LPIPS FVD DINOv2 - 0.276 0.360 0.457 0.382 0.380 0. - 15.55 15.31 21.25 15.69 15.59 13.26 - 0.220 0.149 0.173 0.147 0.147 0.143 CLIP Cons. 0.974 0.973 0.975 0.984 0.980 0.980 0.972 Optical Flow - 41.3 118.5 145.2 157.2 158.8 38.7 5.2 Coarse-Video Guided Generation To further verify the effectiveness and generalization of our method, we leverage it to achieve camera-controlled video generation by treating this task as coarse-video guided generation process. The camera-controlled video generation is: given the first frame, corresponding camera intrinsics, and the pose, we aim to generate video that follows sequence of prescribed camera motions. To achieve this, we first use pretrained 3-D models to render coarse video from the first frame and the prescribed camera motions. This coarse video is low-quality (warped) but can capture camera motion. Then it will serve as the guidance for the subsequent video generation. Dataset. Following previous camera-controlled video generation work, we use DL3DV-10K [26] as our dataset for constructing coarse videos and for evaluation. DL3DV-10K contains annotated data samples, each comprising video with camera intrinsics and camera poses of all frames. We randomly select 160 samples, retain the first 49 frames, and resize them to 720 480 resolution. We use this subset as our dataset. Implementations. We first use DepthPro [2] to extract the depth information of the first frame. Then project it into 3-D point cloud. For the given sequenced 12 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Fig. 5: Qualitative comparisons on the subset of DL3DV-10K. Our method shows better appearance alignment to the ground truth (see highlighted blue boxes). camera motion poses, we can render sequence of corresponding images and concatenate them into the coarse video. The coarse video contains two parts. 1) Valid part: pixels that exist in the given first frame. 2) Invalid part: Pixels that do not exist in the given first frame. For the invalid part, the pixels are filled by nearest-neighbor assignment. All details about obtaining the coarse video are in the Appendix. During sampling, we set the weight function λσ = σα and give these two parts different α. For the valid part, α = 4 and for the invalid part, α = 8. The pretrained model we used is CogVideoX-5b-I2V [53]. For all other hyperparameters, we followed the default settings of previous work [41, 53]. Baselines. We compared our method with 1) Training-based GWTF [3]: learning noise warping based on optical flows to model the motion. We used the official trained model and set its key hyper-parameter degradation value γ to 0.5 and 0.7 as recommended in the original paper. 2) Training-free TTM [41]: leverage start-guided synthesis for two decoupled parts. This method also depends on coarse video. For fairness, we use the same warped coarse as ours. We also took tweak = 4 (tw), tstrong = 8 (ts), and tweak = 4, tstrong = 9 according to the default and recommended in their paper and code. For all other hyperparameters, we follow their default or recommendations. Coarse-guided-Gen 13 Metrics. We evaluated the quality of generated videos at the frame level using Mean Squared Error (MSE ) and LPIPS [57] relative to the ground-truth videos. Additionally, we calculated the distributional difference between the groundtruth videos and the synthetic videos using Frechet Video Distance [48] (FVD). We also calculated the average semantic similarity of each frame between groundtruth videos and the synthetic videos with DINOv2 Distance [33] (DINOv2 ). We used the CLIP similarity [36] between two consistent frames (CLIP Cons.) to evaluate the temporal consistency. For camera motion consistency, we calculated the Mean Squared Error between the RAFT-estimated optical flows [47] of the ground-truth videos and those of the synthetic videos (Optical Flow ). Results. As shown in Table 2, our method achieves the best performance across all metrics except CLIP Cons.. This indicates that our synthetic videos are closest to ground-truth videos and have the best motion consistency. For the CLIP Cons., although our method has the lowest 0.972, we should note that the ground truth only achieves 0.974 on this metric. This indicates that other methods even outperform the ground truth on this metric by sacrificing motion consistency to get quite similar consecutive frames. Additionally, we provide qualitative comparisons in Figure 5. We can see that our method can generate videos with better ground truth alignment and decent image quality. This indicates that our method can effectively generate high-quality videos under coarse video guidance. 5.3 Ablation on Hyperparameter α Since the weight scheduler λσ = σα , the weight of the term can be adjusted by α. To investigate the impact of this key hyperparameter, we ablated it on the super-resolution task (same setting as Section 5.1). For α {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, we report the change curves of FID and LIPIS in Figure 6. When the α is small (e.g., 1), the corresponding weight is large and incorporates an unbearable approximation error. Thus, we can observe that both metrics are quite high. As the α increases, the performance improves since the error is restricted effectively and achieves good performance around α = 5. After that, performance again degraded due to the insufficient traction from small weighted h. Moreover, we also provided the visualizations of synthetic results under various α in Figure 7. We can see that when α is too small (e.g., 1), the image quality is limited (not clear) due to large approximation error. When α is excessively large (e.g., 9), the synthetic image deviates from the ground-truth due to the insufficient guidance of the coarse image. When the α is tempered (e.g., around 5), our method achieves good balance between image quality and guidance adherence. More ablations are in the Appendix. Fig. 6: Ablation study of the α. 14 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Fig. 7: Super-resolution results by using different α. large α leads to small weight, and vice versa (weight scheduler λσ = σα ). 5.4 Compatibility with Flow Matching Models To further verify the generalization ability of our method, we evaluated it on the cameracontrolled video generation task (cf ., Section 5.2) by using flow-based model Wan2.2 [49]. We gave qualitative results of Wan2.2 in Figure 8. We can observe that when the pretrained model is Wan2.2, the synthetic videos also yield de- (even cent synthetic result better than CogVideoX since Wan2.2 is stronger base model) under the guidance of the coarse video. This indicates that our method is compatible with both score-based models like CogVideoX and flow-based models like Wan2.2, showing good generalizations. This indicates that, although our proposed Weighted h-Transform Sampling is based on the score perspective, it can generally be compatible with flow matching and score matching. Fig. 8: Our method is compatible with both scorebased CogVideoX and Flow-based Wan2.2.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we proposed Weighted h-Transform Sampling for coarseguided visual generation. We inject the guidance during the sampling process by designing an function that can approximately drive the generation towards the underlying ideal sample. We further design weight scheduler to restrict the approximation error for better synthetic results. Extensive comparisons and ablation show the generalizations and effectiveness. In the future, probability transition modification mechanisms (represented by h-transform) can be further explored in more general conditional generation applications. Coarse-guided-Gen

sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: This appendix is organized as follows: Section A.1 gives the proof of equivalent marginal distributions between the reversed-SDE and its corresponding PF-ODE. Section A.2 demonstrates the detailed derivation of hx0= (cid:101)y. Section A.3 provides the Weighted h-Transform Inference equation for velocitybased Optimal Transport Flow Matching (OT-FM) and noise-based VariancePreserving SDE (VP-SDE). Section A.4 indicates the implementation details of our method, including how we obtain the coarse videos, text prompts of video generation, and how we set related hyperparameters. Section A.5 provides additional results, including the ablation study of weight function λ and additional qualitative results. Section A.6 shows the extension of our method on the image editing task. 20 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen A.1 Equivalence Proof of Marginal Distributions In this section, we provide the formal proof that the stochastic differential equation (SDE) in Eq. (6) and the ordinary differential equation (ODE) in Eq. (7) share the same marginal distribution. Let qt(xt) denote the marginal probability density of the latent xt at time t. We first write the drift term of the SDE (Eq. (6)) as: µSDE(xt, t) = (xt, t) g2(t)xt log pt(xt) g2(t)hx0=y (A16) The evolution of the probability density qt(xt) for this reverse-time SDE is governed by the Fokker-Planck equation [30] (F-P equation). Taking into account the negative time increment in the reverse process, the corresponding F-P equation is given by: qt 1 2 = (µSDEqt) g2(t)qt (A17) Then, we substitute the mathematical identity qt = (qt) = (qt log qt) into Eq. (A17) yields: qt = (µSDEqt) 1 2 g2(t) (qt log qt) (A18) We can reformulate the equation as standard continuity equation: qt = (cid:20) µSDE + (cid:124) qt (cid:21) (cid:125) 1 2 g2(t) log qt (cid:123)(cid:122) vODE (A19) Eq. (A19) takes the form qt = (vODEqt), which precisely describes the density evolution of deterministic ODE dx = vODEdt. Therefore, the equivalent deterministic drift vODE that shares the same marginal density qt is: vODE = µSDE + 1 g2(t) log qt(xt) (A20) In the context of diffusion models, the goal of introducing the additional term hx0=y is to shift the unconditional score to the target conditional score. By definition, the exact marginal score of the process is structurally related to the unconditional score via: log qt(xt) = xt log pt(xt) + hx0=y (A21) Finally, we substitute the definition of µSDE and the score identity from Eq. (A21) into Eq. (A20): vODE = (cid:2)f (xt, t) g2(t)xt log pt(xt) g2(t)hx0=y g2(t) (xt log pt(xt) + hx0=y) + 1 2 (cid:3) (A22) Coarse-guided-Gen 21 Simplifying the terms, we obtain: vODE = (xt, t) 1 2 g2(t) (xt log pt(xt) + hx0=y) (A23) This derived drift vODE is identical to the drift of the ODE presented in Eq. (7). Since the same continuity equation governs the probability density of the given SDE and this resulting ODE, we conclude that they exhibit the same marginal distribution qt(xt) at all times t. 22 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen A.2 Derivation of hx0= (cid:101)y In this section, we provide the detailed derivation of hx0= (cid:101)y. Based on Eq. (10), we can get the probability density function of pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y): pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) = 1 (2πσ2 )d/2 (cid:18) exp xt αt (cid:101)y2 2σ2 (cid:19) . (A24) Then take the log of both sides of the equation and calculate the gradient: (cid:20) 2 log(2πσ2 ) xtxt αt (cid:101)y2 (cid:21) xt αt (cid:101)y2 2σ2 (A25) = xt log pt(xtx0 = (cid:101)y) = xt 1 2σ2 2(xt αt (cid:101)y) 2σ2 xt αt (cid:101)y σ2 = = = 1 σ2 (αt (cid:101)y xt). Then we substitute it into Eq. (9) and get the final hx0= (cid:101)y, i.e., Eq. (12). In this section, we provide the derivation of the inference equation of v-based Optimal Transport Flow Matching (OT-FM) and ϵ-based Variance-Preserving SDE (VP-SDE). OT-FM with v-prediction. In Flow Matching, the velocity field vθ(xt, t) corresponds to the PF-ODE drift. (A33): (cid:101)ϵ = xt αt (cid:101)y σt (A34) = (cid:101)ϵ, we can substitute this into the inner guidϵθ λσ ((cid:101)ϵ + ϵθ) = ϵθ + λσ ((cid:101)ϵ ϵθ) This reveals the effective guided noise prediction ˆϵθ: ˆϵθ = ϵθ + λσ ((cid:101)ϵ ϵθ) (A35) (A36) Consequently, the guided PF-ODE simplifies elegantly to the standard unguided formulation, driven entirely by the linearly interpolated noise ˆϵθ: dx = (cid:18) αt αt xt (cid:19) dt ˆϵθ σt (A37) Coarse-guided-Gen A.

sections.title: Implementation details
sections.content: Obtaining the Coarse Videos. In Section 5.2, we use 3-D models to render an image into corresponding coarse warped video based on its camera intrinsics and poses. First, we leverage pretrained depth estimation model, DepthPro [2], to get the depth map of the first frame image. Then we reproject this depth map into point cloud and render sequence of images for given camera motion poses. To ensure motion quality, we filter out static or minimal-movement sequences where the camera translation extent is less than 8. To resolve the scale ambiguity of monocular depth maps, we align them with the camera trajectories using SIFT feature matching [29] between the reference frame and subsequent frames (sampled at 5-frame intervals). Matches are refined using k-nearest neighbor search (k = 2) and Lowes ratio test threshold of 0.75. After computing candidate scales via projection constraints, we discard the top and bottom 5% outliers and apply the median value as the final scale. The reference image is warped to target views by unprojecting valid pixels (depth > 0) into 3-D space using the inverse intrinsic matrix and the calibrated depth. These points are transformed into the target camera coordinate system and splatted onto the 2-D image plane. To correctly resolve self-occlusions during rendering, we implement Zbuffering approach by sorting the projected points in descending order of depth. To address deocclusion holes inherently caused by forward splatting, we implement global nearest-neighbor filling strategy utilizing k-d tree built on valid pixel coordinates. The corresponding visibility masks are further refined using morphological opening with 5 5 rectangular kernel to eliminate isolated projection noise and smooth edges. More details can be found in the code. Image-to-Video Prompts Preparation. For prompts generation, we follow the protocol of CogVideoX [53], i.e., leverage the pretrained Vision-Language model GPT-4o [16] to generate caption for the given first frame. These generated prompts will be used as the text condition for subsequent image-to-video generation. The Implementation of Using Wan2.2 for Camera-controlled Video Generation. For Wan2.2 [49], we change the length and resolution of evaluated videos, i.e., frame number from 49 into 81 and the resolution from 720 480 into 832480. During sampling, we set the weight function λσ = σα and give these two parts different α. For the valid part, α = 16 and for the invalid part, α = 20. For other hyperparameters of Wan2.2, we follow the implementations of TTM [41]. Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Table A3: Ablation study of the weight function λ. λ Super-resolution Inpainting FID LPIPS FID LPIPS 49.87 0.369 51.79 0.461 52.80 0.500 81.15 0.433 35.06 0.117 47.99 0.331 0.380 0.466 0.503 0.208 0.209 0.219 )3 40.42 ( )5 40.80 ( )7 39.58 ( σ3 35.02 σ5 33.61 σ7 32. A.5 Additional Results Ablation of the Weight Function. To further explore the influence of the weight function, we ablated it on image super-resolution and inpainting tasks (cf ., Section 5.1). As shown in Table A3, we experimented the λ = ( )α and λ = σα and gave α different values. We can observe that when the weight function λ is σt-related function (last three rows), our method can achieve generally better performance across tasks and metrics. This indicates that our weight function design, which is inspired by the analysis of approximation error (negatively correlated to the noise level σt), is reasonable and effective. Additional Qualitative Results. We provide additional qualitative comparisons with SDEdit [31] (cf ., Section 5.1) on four image restoration tasks in Figure A9. Besides, we provide additional qualitative comparisons with TTM [41] and GWTF [3] (cf ., Section 5.2) on camera-controlled video generation tasks in Figure A10. Moreover, we gave more camera-controlled video generation results with Wan2.2 as the base model in Figure A11. Coarse-guided-Gen 27 Fig. A9: Additional qualitative comparisons with SDEdit on image restoration tasks. 28 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Fig. A10: Additional camera-controlled video generation qualitative comparisons. Coarse-guided-Gen 29 Fig. A11: Additional camera-controlled video generation results using Wan2.2. 30 Y. Wang, Z. Jiang, Z. Wang, L. Chen Table A4: Quantitative comparisons with five baselines for image editing on PIEBench. Bold: best, underline: second best. Method ODE-Inv SDEdit [31] iRFDS [52] FlowEdit [22] FlowAlign [20] Ours Source Consistency Semantic Alignment Distance PSNR LPIPS MSE SSIM CLIP Entire CLIP Edited 0.024 0.691 17.57 0.008 0.747 22.57 0.021 0.738 18.81 23.02 0.007 0.842 25.50 0.053 0.004 0.879 25.98 0.097 0.004 0.819 0.074 0.036 0.069 0.036 0.028 0.017 24.57 24.56 25.12 25.98 25.28 25.41 21.73 21.95 21.95 22.81 22.00 22.37 0.240 0.119 0.191 0. A.6 Image Editing To further explore the generalization ability of our method, we conducted the experiments on text-based image editing task, i.e., given source image, source prompt, and target prompt, we want to edit the source image into target image according to the description of the target prompt. Specifically, our method use the source image as the (cid:101)y and the sθ is the predicted score by pretrained text-to-image models that adopt the target prompt as the condition. We set the sampling timestep as 50, and the target guidance scale was set to 13.5, and the weight function is λσ = σt. We compared our method with five state-of-the-art flow-based editing methods that were implemented with SD-3-medium [11]: 1) ODE inversion (ODEInv ), which gets the inversion by using the Euler Solver and denoises with the target prompt. 2) SDEdit [31], which adds random noise and then denoises with the target prompt. 3) iRFDS [52], which is SDS-based editing method. 4) FlowEdit [22] and 5) FlowAlign [20] are two inversion-free editing methods. The results of these baselines are from FlowAlign [20]. We evaluated on PIEBench [17] and reported the seven commonly used metrics to evaluate both source consistency (Structure Distance, Background PSNR, Background LPIPS, Background MSE, Background SSIM ) and Semantic Alignment (Entire CLIP Score and Edited CLIP Score). As shown in Table A4, our method can outperform the ODE-Inv, SDEdit and iRFDS. Meanwhile, we can perform competitively against their current strong methods, e.g., FlowEdit and FlowAlign. As highlight, our method achieves such performance even without incorporating the source prompt as the prior. We also gave qualitative comparisons in Figure A12. We can see that our results show superior editing performance (better balance between the source consistency and target semantic alignment) than baselines. Coarse-guided-Gen 31 Fig. A12: Text-based image editing comparisons with three baselines on PIE-Bench
